Perfect — thanks for the details. Looping Ben and Laura in as requested.

Ben / Laura — Ethan confirmed a target of ~1.2B tokens/mo with a 6-month ramp (expect ~20% MoM). Requested outputs:
- Phased slab simulation (start lower, step-up bands over 6 months).
- An annual prepay option with an additional 6% off the blended rate.
- Rollover language that results in invoice-level credits (partial rollover within the quarter).

Ben: can you run the phased slab and attach a tidy proposal (numbers + a short note on margin impact)? Laura: can you flag any billing/legal constraints for invoice-level credits vs. post-invoice adjustments?

I told Ethan we'll aim to have a polished term sheet + phased slab by early next week.

Thanks,
Aditya



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Mar 2026 09:11:00 -0700
Subject: Phased slab proposal for SaaSFlow (RD-2026-2319)

Ethan / Aditya —

Attached is the phased_slab_proposal.pdf with two commercial options. High level:
Option A (phased commit, monthly billing):
- Months 1-2: committed 400M tokens/month, rate $0.00018/token
- Months 3-4: committed 800M tokens/month, rate $0.00015/token
- Months 5-6+: committed 1.2B tokens/month, rate $0.00013/token
- Effective blended rate over 6 months (assuming your 20% MoM ramp): $0.000148/token
- Rollover: unused committed tokens may be credited on next invoice within the quarter (credit applied as line-item).

Option B (annual prepay):
- Single 12-month prepay for expected 1.2B tokens/mo at $0.000122/token (6% additional discount vs Option A blended)
- No monthly rollover (prepay model replaces rollover)

Margin note: Option A preserves flexibility; Option B gives best unit economics but increases revenue recognition complexity and requires prepay PO.

If this aligns, we can convert Option A to a term sheet with explicit credit calculations and sample invoice formatting.

Attachment: phased_slab_proposal.pdf

—Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Pricing, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com



From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>, SaaSFlow Procurement <procurements@saasflow.io>
Date: Sat, 14 Mar 2026 10:32:00 -0700
Subject: Billing & terms considerations for phased commit (SaaSFlow)

Team / Ethan —

A few quick finance/legal items to note before finalizing the term sheet:
- Billing cadence: Net30 standard. If SaaSFlow requests Net45, we need C-level approval for increased days outstanding.
- Credits vs adjustments: We can do invoice-level credits for rollover within the quarter. Credits will appear as negative line-items and will be applied to the following invoice. We cannot do retroactive refund checks beyond the quarter without senior approval.
- PO & prepay: For Option B (prepay), procurement must issue a PO and we require an internal revenue ops ticket to handle prepayment accounting.
- SOW / signature: attached a draft SOW with sample language for committal and rollback clauses. Please review and mark up.

Attachment: SaaSFlow-SOW-draft.docx

I'll take the lead on the SOW redline once the commercial option is chosen.

Best,
Laura Bennett
Director, Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
laura_bennett@redwood.com



From: Ethan Walters <ethan@saasflow.io>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: SaaSFlow Procurement <procurements@saasflow.io>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Mar 2026 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SaaSFlow: slab pricing simulation & commit phasing

All —

Thanks for the clear options. We prefer Option A (phased commit) with the invoice-level rollover credits as you described. We will:
- Issue a PO for the initial two-month minimum (400M tokens/mo) to start the ramp.
- Accept Net30.
- Request the term sheet + SOW redline so our legal can review; aim to sign next week.

Can you send a concise term sheet (one page) that summarizes: option selected, monthly commit steps, rates, rollover mechanics, and billing cadence? Also propose a 30-minute signature readiness call tomorrow or Tuesday so we can expedite.

Ethan

title: RFI response: Assimilation roadmap and interoperability acceptance checklist
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Megan Li <megan.li@aurorafin.com>, Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@aurorafin.com>
Cc: procurement@aurorafin.com
Date: Mon, 09 Mar 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: RFI response: Assimilation roadmap and interoperability acceptance checklist

Hi Megan / Oliver,

Thanks for the detailed RFI — attached is our first-pass assimilation roadmap and a lightweight interoperability acceptance checklist to help Aurora align internal teams. Key highlights:

- Proposed 6-week onboarding cadence (pre-prod shadowing -> staged canary -> full cutover). See tab \"Milestones\" in the attached roadmap.
- Canary plan: 3-week ramp with percent-based traffic shifts (1%, 5%, 20%, 60%, 100%) and telemetry gates at each step.
- Integration touchpoints: VPC peering (or PrivateLink), SAML SSO + SCIM provisioning, and mutual TLS for service-to-service calls.
- Data controls: model version pinning, per-route token redaction, and optional prefix caching to limit PII exposure.

Items we need from Aurora to finalize timeline:
1) Preferred canary windows (UTC range) and maintenance blackout days
2) VPC CIDR for peering/testing and NAT/Egress constraints
3) Decision on SSO vs API key for service accounts (we support both)
4) Any runbook owners for Aurora (name/email) so we can include them in the kick-off

Attachments included: redwood_assimilation_roadmap_v1.xlsx (timeline + gating), integration_acceptance_criteria.pdf (acceptance checklist), network_requirements_summary.pdf (ports & endpoints).

If helpful, we can run a 30-minute walkthrough of the worksheet tomorrow (Tue). Proposed times: 10:00–10:30am PT or 2:00–2:30pm PT.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0192

---
Attachments:
- redwood_assimilation_roadmap_v1.xlsx (spreadsheet, 5 sheets)
- integration_acceptance_criteria.pdf (PDF, 4 pages)
- network_requirements_summary.pdf (PDF, 2 pages)

From: Megan Li <megan.li@aurorafin.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@aurorafin.com>
Cc: procurement@aurorafin.com
Date: Mon, 09 Mar 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFI response: Assimilation roadmap and interoperability acceptance checklist

Thanks Ava — this is very helpful. A few clarifications from the Aurora integration team so we can finalize dependencies: 

- Canary windows: we prefer low-traffic windows for the heavier shifts. Our default maintenance window is 01:00–04:00 UTC on Sundays. Are your telemetry gates timezone-agnostic (they're just thresholds we can evaluate in UTC)?
- Data residency: Aurora requires customer data to remain in us-west-2 for this initial rollout. Can you confirm the hosted model instances can be pinned to that region and that logs/audit trails are stored in our dedicated S3 bucket?
- Auth: procurement prefers SAML for human SSO and short-lived API keys for machine/service accounts. Need confirmation on SCIM support for user provisioning.
- Acceptance criteria: add a bullet to the checklist for latency SLOs (p95 <= 350ms for standard generation route) and error-rate threshold (<0.5% 5xx across 24h).

Also attaching a short set of Aurora security questions we still need answered (I will send the doc separately to procurement). For the timeline: can you split out the \"pre-prod shadowing\" phase into two weeks (1 week shadowing, 1 week parallel validate)? That gives us time to exercise our egress guards.

We can do the walkthrough tomorrow at 2:00pm PT. I'll invite Oliver and our integration lead (David Park) if available.

Thanks,
Megan Li
Integration Lead, Aurora Financial
megan.li@aurorafin.com

Thanks for the walkthrough earlier today — great alignment. Attached are the updated artifacts we discussed:

- redwood_assimilation_roadmap_v1.xlsx (updated):
  * Week 0: Kickoff & network provisioning (VPC peering / PrivateLink + S3 bucket access)
  * Weeks 1–2: Pre-prod (Week 1 shadowing, Week 2 parallel validate)
  * Weeks 3–5: Canary ramp (1% -> 5% -> 20% -> 60% -> 100%, gating windows listed in sheet)
  * Week 6: Final acceptance and handover

- integration_acceptance_criteria.pdf (updated):
  * Added operational sign-off checklist and explicit SLOs (p95 <= 350ms, 5xx < 0.5% 24h).
  * Added security gating: SOC2 evidence pack checklist and data residency confirmation.

- runbook_preview.md: Excerpt of canary gating runbook with rollback triggers:
  * Automatic rollback on sustained >0.5% 5xx for 30m OR p95 degradation >150ms vs baseline for 15m.
  * Manual rollback if functional regressions discovered by Aurora validation team with documented repro steps.

Region pinning: Markus confirmed we can reserve capacity in us-west-2 and route Aurora's tenant to instances in that region. We can also export audit logs to the S3 bucket (we will need a dedicated IAM role ARN from Aurora). HSM integration for customer-managed keys is available as part of Private deployments; we can include that in the SOW addendum.

SCIM and SAML: Laura confirms SCIM provisioning is supported for user lifecycle; SAML 2.0 works for UI login and we emit short-lived JWTs for SDK/service flows. We'll include a sample SAML metadata XML in the shared drive.

Subject: RFI response: Assimilation roadmap and interoperability acceptance checklist

Hi Megan / Oliver,

Thanks for the detailed RFI — attached is our first-pass assimilation roadmap and a lightweight interoperability acceptance checklist to help Aurora align internal teams. Key highlights:

- Proposed 6-week onboarding cadence (pre-prod shadowing -> staged canary -> full cutover). See tab \"Milestones\" in the attached roadmap.
- Canary plan: 3-week ramp with percent-based traffic shifts (1%, 5%, 20%, 60%, 100%) and telemetry gates at each step.
- Integration touchpoints: VPC peering (or PrivateLink), SAML SSO + SCIM provisioning, and mutual TLS for service-to-service calls.
- Data controls: model version pinning, per-route token redaction, and optional prefix caching to limit PII exposure.

Items we need from Aurora to finalize timeline:
1) Preferred canary windows (UTC range) and maintenance blackout days
2) VPC CIDR for peering/testing and NAT/Egress constraints
3) Decision on SSO vs API key for service accounts (we support both)
4) Any runbook owners for Aurora (name/email) so we can include them in the kick-off

Attachments included: redwood_assimilation_roadmap_v1.xlsx (timeline + gating), integration_acceptance_criteria.pdf (acceptance checklist), network_requirements_summary.pdf (ports & endpoints).

If helpful, we can run a 30-minute walkthrough of the worksheet tomorrow (Tue). Proposed times: 10:00–10:30am PT or 2:00–2:30pm PT.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0192

Subject: Re: RFI response: Assimilation roadmap and interoperability acceptance checklist

Thanks Ava — this is very helpful. A few clarifications from the Aurora integration team so we can finalize dependencies: 

- Canary windows: we prefer low-traffic windows for the heavier shifts. Our default maintenance window is 01:00–04:00 UTC on Sundays. Are your telemetry gates timezone-agnostic (they're just thresholds we can evaluate in UTC)?
- Data residency: Aurora requires customer data to remain in us-west-2 for this initial rollout. Can you confirm the hosted model instances can be pinned to that region and that logs/audit trails are stored in our dedicated S3 bucket?
- Auth: procurement prefers SAML for human SSO and short-lived API keys for machine/service accounts. Need confirmation on SCIM support for user provisioning.
- Acceptance criteria: add a bullet to the checklist for latency SLOs (p95 <= 350ms for standard generation route) and error-rate threshold (<0.5% 5xx across 24h).

Also attaching a short set of Aurora security questions we still need answered (I will send the doc separately to procurement). For the timeline: can you split out the \"pre-prod shadowing\" phase into two weeks (1 week shadowing, 1 week parallel validate)? That gives us time to exercise our egress guards.

We can do the walkthrough tomorrow at 2:00pm PT. I'll invite Oliver and our integration lead (David Park) if available.

Thanks,
Megan Li
Integration Lead, Aurora Financial
megan.li@aurorafin.com

title: ExpoCloud West — lead onramp & consent routing
preview:
From: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 09:14:00 -0800
Subject: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for coordinating with ExpoTech on lead ingestion for ExpoCloud West (Mar 2-4). Two quick items before we lock the integration:

1) Formats: our badge scanners export a nightly CSV with the following columns: badge_id, first_name, last_name, email (optional), company, title, checkin_time. We can also include a hashed email field instead of plain email.
2) Consent: we need to make sure your HubSpot sync preserves the opt-in state and that event attendees receive the correct marketing preference.

I've attached the event spec and a sample export. Can you confirm whether Redwood can:
- Accept hashed emails as the dedupe key if email is present;
- Push contact creation to HubSpot with a source property \"expo_cloudwest\"; and
- Store raw scan exports for a short retention window for debugging?

We have a technical ops lead on our side (expo-ops@expotech.com) who can provide sample outputs if helpful.

Appreciate any notes on data retention and consent language we should surface on the onsite forms.

Best,
Sara Kline
Senior Events Manager
ExpoTech Events

Attachment: event_spec_ExpoCloudWest.pdf (pdf, 240KB)

From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 10:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Hi Sara — thanks for the clear summary and the spec. Looping in Ben (field ops) and Grace (privacy/compliance). A few quick answers and next steps: 

- Yes, we can accept a hashed_email field as the primary dedupe key; when plain email is present we prefer to store both but only push the hashed value to HubSpot unless the customer explicitly opts in.
- We can create contacts in HubSpot with a \"lead_source\" property set to \"expo_cloudwest\" and tag the contact with campaign id when available.
- Short-term retention (30 days) for raw CSVs is workable for debugging; we prefer not to keep raw PII longer than necessary.

Can Ben outline the suggested HubSpot object mapping and dedupe rules? Grace — can you share the minimum consent language we should require on the onsite form so Sara can update their scanner UI?

If it helps, I'm free for a 30-min sync Thursday 02/12 11:00 PT.

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Product Manager, Events Integrations
Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood_onramp_checklist.pdf (pdf, 85KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
Cc: Diego Fernandez <diego@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 14:37:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Thanks — dug into the sample spec. High-level plan (technical):

1) Ingest: ExpoTech SFTP drops nightly CSV to our ingest bucket. We run a lightweight ETL that normalizes columns. Preferred input columns (we'll accept variants): badge_id, first_name, last_name, email, hashed_email, company, title, checkin_time, device_id.

2) Dedupe: If email is present, our dedupe uses email (normalize lowercasing). If only hashed_email is present, we use hashed_email + badge_id as a composite key for temporary staging, and we recommend mapping hashed_email to HubSpot "email" only if the customer has opted in and provided consent mapping.

3) HubSpot mapping (Contact + Engagement):
- Contact fields: firstname -> first_name, lastname -> last_name, company -> company, jobtitle -> title, email -> email (or hashed_email as fallback).
- Contact property: lead_source = expo_cloudwest; event_date = 2026-03-02
- Engagement: create a timeline event 'expo_checkin' with metadata {badge_id, device_id, checkin_time}.

4) Idempotency: we will use contact.external_id set to badge_id for event-specific dedupe if that is zone-specific and should not overwrite global contact records.

I've attached a starter mapping CSV for your scanner team. Diego, can you validate the device_id field mapping and confirm whether our parser will accept checkin_time in ISO8601?

Ben Carter
Field Ops Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: badge_field_map_v1.csv (csv, 4KB)

From: Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
To: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 08:55:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Hi all — quick compliance notes and proposed onsite consent language.

Consent summary (minimum acceptable):
- Required copy to display near the scanner/button: \"By providing your badge or contact details, you agree that ExpoTech and Redwood Inference may process your contact information for event follow-up and marketing. You may opt out at any time.\"
- On ingestion: mark a boolean property contact.marketing_consent = true/false. Only when true should we push plain email to HubSpot and add to marketing lists.

Data retention:
- Raw CSVs with PII: retain for 30 days for troubleshooting; automatically delete after retention expiry.
- Derived/hashes for dedupe: may be kept for 12 months to support de-duplication across events, unless the customer requests deletion.

Security: ensure SFTP uploads are over TLS with key-based auth; transport-level encryption only (no PII in logs). For SOC 2 alignment we need an audit trail of pushes to HubSpot and proof that hashed_email cannot be reversed by Redwood.

Attachment: consent-language-v2.docx (docx, 6KB)

Thanks,
Grace O'Connor
Privacy & Compliance Lead
Redwood Inference

From: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 14 Feb 2026 16:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Thanks everyone — all good on our side. We can generate hashed_email (SHA256) in the scanner firmware and will include plain email only when attendees explicitly opt in.

I've attached a fresh sample CSV that matches Ben's mapping. I'm also sending a short DocuSign to sign the data-transfer addendum that specifies 30-day raw retention and HubSpot push behavior. Link: https://www.docusign.net/EXTERNAL/1234abcdef

Attachment: expo_sample_export.csv (csv, 12KB)

—Sara

On a separate note: will your on-site team be able to provide a live retry if an SFTP drop fails? We want a quick SLA window for the day-of support.


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 11:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Quick internal summary and next steps:

Decisions made:
- Ingest: accept nightly SFTP CSV; will accept hashed_email SHA256 and optional plaintext email only when marketing_consent=true.
- HubSpot: create contact with lead_source=expo_cloudwest and create engagement 'expo_checkin'.
- Retention: raw CSV retained 30 days, hashed/dedupe data retained 12 months.
- Security: SFTP w/ key auth; audit logs for HubSpot pushes.

Action items:
- Ben: finalize parser to accept checkin_time ISO8601 and device_id (ETA 02/20). See badge_field_map_v1.csv for mapping.
- Diego: implement SFTP monitor and day-of retry workflow; provide on-call contact for 03/02-03/04.
- Grace: approve final consent copy and sign off on DTA once DocuSign is completed.
- Aditya: coordinate smoke test week of 02/23 with ExpoTech (Sara) and run a dry ingest.

I'll post the final runbook in the Drive folder and link to the DocuSign once signed.

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Product Manager, Events Integrations
Redwood Inference

title: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 15:03:00 +0000
Subject: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Sarah — thanks for the call earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have a concrete test plan we can run next week.

Recap (what we heard)
- Primary evaluation lens: unit cost per 1M tokens under realistic production mix (short chat messages + occasional long generations).
- Constraints: sub-150ms median latency target, p95 < 400ms, and ability to preserve conversational state (KV cache) for prompt reuse.
- Hyperscaler interest: cheaper headline price but unclear how caching, batching, and model variants affect real-world effective cost.

Redwood baseline (ballpark numbers for planning)
- Hosted rwd-small / rwd-medium options: baseline modeled cost ~ $25–28 per 1M tokens at low batching (1–4) and no cache.
- With continuous batching + conservative 30% prefix cache hit: effective cost falls to ~$17–19 per 1M tokens.
- Dedicated/reserved pool (pinned capacity) can push that to ~$12–15 per 1M tokens for sustained steady throughput given committed GPUs and quantized kernels.

Hyperscaler claim (from call): $15 per 1M tokens. Open questions: what batch sizes and latency targets underpin that number? Do they expose KV/prefix caching (transparent to app) or require custom infra?

Proposed measurable experiments (what we want to run)
1) 100k token warmup run (simulate ramp) — measure median/p95/p99 latencies, tcp time, and token throughput.
2) 1M token economic pass with three axes: batch sizes [1, 4, 16], cache hit rates [0%, 30%, 60%] (we'll seed prefix cache where possible), and model variant [fp16, int8].
3) Failure/fallback resilience: induce transient GPU saturation and confirm automated fallback latency/regression.

Deliverables from Redwood for the test
- Test harness (we provide a script that emits the token mix and collects per-request token counts + latency).
- Precomputed cost model spreadsheet (attached) with per-step assumptions and sensitivity knobs.
- 2-week MAP PDF with timing and responsibilities (attached).

What we need from you
- A representative 1,000–2,000 prompt sample (anonymized) and approximate concurrency target (RPS) — helps pick batch windows.
- Any constraints on where tests must run (cloud region, VPC peering only, or can we run hosted?).

Proposed timing
- Prep this week (get prompts + region) — run experiments early next week — deliver consolidated results + recommendation within 48 hours of finish.

Next step: if this looks good I’ll share the test harness and put together the low-friction access keys for a short hosted canary (or provision a small dedicated pool if you prefer to test on reserved capacity).

Attachments: redwood-econ-scenarios-1M.xlsx, mutual-action-plan-hyperstack.pdf

—
Aditya Rao
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com | +1 555-0184




From: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@hyperstack.com
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 18:21:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Thanks, Aditya — this is exactly the level of detail we need. A few quick clarifications: 

- We'll provide a 1,200-sample anonymized prompt set (mix of short chat ~75% and long generations ~25%) and target steady-state concurrency ~35 RPS.
- Region: US-east preferred; we can accept hosted but will need the option to run via our cloud account if we replicate later.
- Procurement is copied to coordinate any temporary testing agreement/credits.

Two asks from our side: can you include an explicit latency-to-cost sensitivity (e.g., if we must hit 100ms median instead of 150ms how does cost change)? And can you call out any model-level quality deltas you expect across fp16 vs int8?

If the attached MAP includes roles, please add our infra lead Tim (tim@cloudfast.io) — he’ll validate network/peering constraints.

I’m available to kick off the test planning call Friday morning (US ET) or Monday.

—
Sarah Ng
Head of AI | Hyperstack Inc.
sarah@hyperstack.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Feb 2026 09:10:00 +0000
Subject: RE: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix (internal)

Looping internal notes here so we’re aligned before we commit credits: 

- Pricing/commercial: recommend offering a short-term test credit covering the hosted run up to 1.2M tokens plus a 25% evaluation discount on any Dedicated trial capacity (covering 2 weeks). Put the numbers in the MAP so procurement has a clear ask.
- Technical ask: push to run both hosted and a dedicated canary (if they have a short-lived reserved box) — that shows the range of effective costs. Hyperscalers often quote peak-efficiency numbers that assume sustained single-model throughput; our strength is autoscaling + graceful fallbacks and cache effectiveness on real traffic.
- Differentiators to highlight in test report: prefix/KV cache hit amplification, continuous batching with latency SLO knobs, and model-variant fallbacks (auto switch to quantized runtime without client changes).

I’ll draft a short pricing appendix for the MAP and hand off to you, Aditya, to include in the PDF.

—Ben
VP, Sales | Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>, Tim <tim@cloudfast.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, procurement@hyperstack.com
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 08:04:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Sarah / Tim — thanks for confirming. Attached are: 1) redwood-econ-scenarios-1M.xlsx (cost model with sensitivity knobs for latency, batch size, and cache hit rate), and 2) mutual-action-plan-hyperstack.pdf (roles, timeline, and the credit offer Ben mentioned).

Quick answers to your asks:
- Latency-to-cost sensitivity: included in the workbook — short story: moving median target from 150ms -> 100ms increases effective cost by ~18–30% in many mixed workloads because it constrains batching windows and reduces achievable cache effectiveness. It’s in the "Latency Sensitivity" tab.
- FP16 vs INT8: we show expected per-token throughput and a quality note — INT8 can reduce cost by ~20–35% vs fp16 for generative workloads but expect a small delta on rare tokens; we recommend using eval prompts to validate quality prior to sweeping quantization at scale.

Proposed immediate schedule:
- Tue 17 Feb: receive prompt set + region confirmation.
- Wed 18 Feb: Redwood turnkey run (hosted) — 100k warmup then 1M economic pass.
- Thu 19 Feb: analysis and joint readout + recommendation (hosted vs dedicated decision).

Tim — can you confirm if Hyperstack wants the dedicated run to be provisioned in their cloud account or if a hosted dedicated sandbox is easier for the initial test? If you prefer the cloud account, we’ll share infra runbook and IAM scopes needed.

Link to MAP + HubSpot record: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/1234567/company/98765

Regards,
Aditya


From: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Tim <tim@cloudfast.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, procurement@hyperstack.com
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 09:12:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Sounds good — we’ll send the prompt set today and confirm US-east. Tim prefers we provision in our cloud account for the dedicated run (so he can validate networking), but hosted first is fine.

Friday morning call doesn’t work for us; Monday 17 Feb 14:00 US ET (18:00 UTC) works to kick off prep and sync on keys.

Thanks — looking forward to the test.

—
Sarah

title: Trajectory plan: pricing gates & evaluation milestone approval — Skyline Data
preview:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>
To: Evan Li <evan@skylinedata.com>
Cc: Dana Kim <dana.kim@skylinedata.com>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (first draft)

Evan / Dana,

Thanks again for the time yesterday. As promised, attached is a first-pass Mutual Action Plan (PDF) that ties the evaluation milestones to commercial gates. High level:

- Week 0–2: Platform onboarding + smoke tests (no commit).
- Week 3–5: Pilot eval with 50k tokens/day — customer trial pricing (trial credits).
- Gate A (end week 5): If pilot passes acceptance criteria, customer commits to 3-month reserved capacity at 40% discount vs on-demand.
- Week 6–12: Ramp to target throughput and P95 latency SLOs; incremental discounts unlocked at 60% and 80% utilization checkpoints.

Key asks for you today: please confirm the acceptance criteria in section 2 (smoke/perf thresholds) and confirm the preferred billing entity for the contract.

I have also attached a pricing spreadsheet (iteration v1) but expect to iterate once we lock the checkpoint definitions.

Next step: if you’re comfortable, I can circulate an internal commercial note and a revised pricing iteration for approval by our sales finance and deals desk.

Best,
Aisha
Aisha Rahman | Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference
Attachments: 2026-01-15_SKY-line_MAP_v2.pdf (MAP, PDF); pricing_iteration_v3.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Evan Li <evan@skylinedata.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 11:04:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (first draft)

Aisha—

Thanks for the write-up and the draft MAP. Quick clarifications from our side:

- Acceptance criteria: does the pilot acceptance require both 95th pct latency < 200ms AND model quality delta within 3% vs our baseline? We need clarity for our internal signoff.
- Billing entity: procurement wants a 3rd-party PO to Redwood, not an AWS resale.
- Discount triggers: procurement prefers a single ramp discount at Gate A rather than multiple checkpoints to simplify approvals.

If you can send a revised pricing that shows a single step discount at the commit (and separates trial credits from reserved capacity pricing), we can route to procurement next week.

Evan
Evan Li | CTO, Skyline Data


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 16 Jan 2026 09:18:00 -08:00
Subject: Fwd: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (Skyline) — commercial ask

Marissa / Ben — forwarding Evan's note. They want: single-step discount at commit (Gate A) and clarified acceptance criteria. Proposed approach: keep MAP milestones as-is for engineering acceptance checks, but compress commercial discounts to a single committed-discount at Gate A with a small ramp bonus (one-time credit) if they hit 80% utilization by week 10.

Proposed numbers (quick):
- Pilot credits: $12k (one-time, applied during evaluation).
- Commit: 3-month reserved pool 40% off on-demand, minimum monthly $18k (commit).
- Ramp bonus: one-time $5k credit if utilization target reached by week 10.

 asks:
1) Marissa — can deals desk sign off on 40% + $5k ramp credit as a standard special? What approvals do we need?
2) Ben — are there any technical caveats for the acceptance criteria (latency vs quality gating) that will make the eval ambiguous?

I want to move a revised pricing packet + MAP (single discount variant) to procurement by Monday.

Thanks,
Aisha

--- Forwarded message ---
(Attachments: pricing_iteration_v3.xlsx)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 19 Jan 2026 08:56:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Trajectory plan + proposed MAP (Skyline) — commercial ask

Aisha —

40% + $5k ramp credit is acceptable from a commercial standpoint for a mid-market deal with clear acceptance criteria, but we should:

- Add a defined expiration (30 days) on the offer to avoid open-ended approvals.
- Put the $5k as a usage credit applied in month 2 rather than an upfront rebate; that helps accounting.
- Require procurement to sign DocuSign that confirms billing entity and PO flow before we turn on reserved capacity.

I’ll pre-clear this with deals desk/finance — no material legal redlines expected on standard terms. If procurement pushes for additional SLAs, escalate to me before legal review.

Approve to proceed to a commercial revision and DocuSign.

Marissa
VP, Commercial
Redwood Inference

Cc: Dana Kim <dana.kim@skylinedata.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Skyline Data Procurement <procurement@skylinedata.com>
Date: Thu, 29 Jan 2026 14:02:00 -08:00
Subject: Revised MAP + single-step commercial offer (requested)

Evan / Dana —

Per our internal approvals, please find the attached updated MAP and commercial packet. Summary of changes since my first draft:

- Commercial: single-step commit discount at Gate A — 40% off on-demand for a 3-month reserved pool, $18k minimum monthly commitment.
- Pilot: $12k evaluation credits applied during the pilot window.
- Ramp bonus: $5k usage credit applied in month 2 if utilization >= 80% by week 10.
- Expiration: this offer is valid for 30 days from the date of this email.

Acceptance criteria (engineering):
1) P95 inference latency < 200ms measured on the target sequence mix.
2) Model quality delta within 3% of baseline on the evaluation prompt set.
3) No critical security or data residency blockers remain open.

Next steps for Skyline to progress:
- Confirm acceptance criteria and billing entity (reply OK).
- Procurement to execute DocuSign (attached).
- Once DocuSign is complete, Redwood will provision the reserved capacity and apply pilot credits.

Best,
Aisha

From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>

Hi Emily,

Thanks again for the time yesterday — following up with the proposed evaluation roadmap and the throughput targets we discussed. Attached is a working MAP (finco_map_v1.xlsx) and a short summary of the initial performance expectations so procurement has a sense of unit economics. Quick highlights: 

- Target sustained throughput: 500 QPS (inference requests) for the canonical payments use case
- Tokens/sec: target 20k tokens/sec steady-state for a 256-token average response
- Concurrency target: 50 concurrent request slots with tail-latency SLOs
- TTFT target: <= 75ms median; 95th < 250ms for the committed config
- Unit economics (hosted list-price estimate): ~$220 per 1M tokens; dedicated/reserved pricing TBD

Action items I propose for next week:

2) Provide 95th and 99th latency histograms + tokens/sec breakouts (Ben/Marcus)

Quick note on logistics — I can spin up the FinCo test harness on Dedicated Catalyst-8 (8xA100-style) tomorrow and run an initial 6-hour ramp to steady-state followed by a 2-hour steady run. Plan:

- Workload: FinCo prompt set (avg resp ~256 tokens; 60% short, 40% long)
- Ramp: 0 -> target QPS over 30 minutes to capture warm-up effects
- Metrics captured: tokens/sec, sustained QPS, median/95/99 latencies, TTFT, mem/KV cache hit rate
- Expected initial numbers (estimate based on similar workloads): tokens/sec ~18k-21k; sustained QPS 420-500; concurrency 35-50; 95th ~200-260ms; 99th could range 300-500ms depending on network tail.
- Rough costing estimate (on-demand): $0.22 per 1k tokens => ~$220 per 1M tokens (list). With a 30% reserved commitment, estimate drops to ~$154 / 1M tokens.

I'll attach the latency plots and the run plan after the first run (latency_plots_qps.pdf). If Procurement needs cost per million in a line item table I can export from the MAP spreadsheet.

Ben
SRE Lead, Redwood Inference

Attachments: latency_plots_qps.pdf (initial run plan)


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@finco.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 11 Jan 2026 08:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Evaluation roadmap & throughput commitments for Q1 MAP

Emily — attaching an updated MAP (finco_map_v1.xlsx) with concrete commitments from our side and a short results summary from Ben's first run: 

Summary of run (6h warm+2h steady):
- Sustained QPS observed: 450 (steady window averaged over 2h)
- Average tokens/sec: 18,000
- Median TTFT: 60ms; 95th latency: 210ms; 99th latency: 360ms
- Concurrency: 40 active slots during steady window
- Observed unit cost (on-demand): ~$220 / 1M tokens (matches estimate); projected reserved pricing ~ $150-160 / 1M tokens after commit

Committed actions in MAP:
1) Redwood to run a full 24-hour sustained throughput validation (Ben) — target date 13 Jan
2) Redwood to export 95/99 latency histograms + per-route token breakdowns for FinCo prompts (Marcus)
3) FinCo procurement to review the SOW stub and confirm PO timeline (Emily/procurement)
4) Final acceptance criteria: sustain >= 420 QPS for 24h with 99th latency < 400ms and unit cost <= $200 / 1M tokens (reserved pricing applied)

If procurement confirms the window, we'll start the 24-hour run on Tue 13 Jan and share the consolidated report by EOD Thu 15 Jan. Meeting remains Tue 13 Jan 11:00 PT.

Best,
Aditya

Attachments: finco_map_v1.xlsx


From: FinCo Procurement <procurement@finco.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
Cc: Emily Zhang <emily@finco.com>, Jared Cole <jared@cloudops.io>
Date: Tue, 13 Jan 2026 11:00:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Evaluation roadmap & throughput commitments for Q1 MAP

Aditya / Team — procurement has reviewed the MAP and the acceptance criteria. We can proceed with the 24-hour validation run subject to the following: 

- Please provide a one-page SOW stub and a draft PO by EOD Wed 14 Jan
- Network/VPC peering will be provisioned on our side on Mon 12 Jan; confirm Redwood needs any special IPSec config
- We require a cost table that shows list price and reserved discount for 1M/10M/50M token bands

Once we have the SOW and the cost table we will confirm the PO timeline.

Regards,
FinCo Procurement


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@finco.com>, FinCo Procurement <procurement@finco.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 15 Jan 2026 16:45:00 -0800
Subject: Final benchmark report and MAP update — sustained validation complete

Team,

We completed the 24-hour sustained validation window (24 Jan run started/ended as scheduled). Summary of final validated metrics and updated unit economics: 

Final validated performance (24h steady window):
- Sustained QPS (steady): 420
- Average tokens/sec: 16,800
- Median TTFT: 65ms; 95th latency: 245ms; 99th latency: 320ms
- Concurrency sustained: 38 active slots

Notes: tail latency improved vs initial noisy windows; the 99th settled around 320ms once KV cache warmed and request shaping was applied. We saw periodic spikes correlated with the FinCo long responses (top 10% of requests).

Updated unit economics (reserved capacity pricing applied):
- Effective cost (after 30% reserved discount + 10% volume rebate estimate): ~$190 per 1M tokens
- Projected monthly spend at target steady-state (420 QPS, avg 256 tokens, 30 days): ~ $77k/mo (reserve-adjusted) — details in attached MAP spreadsheet.

Attachments: finco_map_v1.xlsx, latency_plots_qps.pdf, redwood_nda_signed.pdf

Next steps / asks: 
1) Emily/procurement: please review the attached SOW stub and cost table and confirm PO send-by date (requested by 2026-01-22). DocuSign link: https://app.docusign.com/s/finco-sow-2026
2) Redwood: propose a technical onboarding week (2 days) after PO to finalize VPC peering and routing
3) We'll schedule a short wrap-up call for 2026-01-23 to review run artifacts and sign-off checklist.

If procurement is comfortable with the numbers we'll convert the MAP to the contract language and move to the dedicated reservation.

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
redwood.ai

title: Reserved commit sizing: throughput guarantees vs burst credits for HorizonBio
preview:
From: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: HorizonBio Procurement <procurement@horizonbio.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T09:13:00-08:00
Subject: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Alex,

Thanks for taking this. Quick background: HorizonBio is evaluating Redwood Dedicated for our clinical-genomics assistant. We expect long contexts (mostly 8k tokens), mixed generation (summaries + structured outputs), and a variable request pattern. Here's our rough profile: 
- Average requests: 60/min sustained
- Peak requests: 220/min for 6–12 minute windows (concurrent users ~40–60)
- Avg tokens per request (input+output): ~3k
- Latency target: p95 < 1.5s, p99 < 2.5s

We need guidance on:
1) Recommended GPU pool (type and #) for a reserved commit that meets the latency targets for sustained and peak traffic
2) How burst credits or a burst pool would work with a reserved commit (tradeoffs, costs)
3) How context length / KV cache affects sizing and whether caching reduces commit needs
4) What discounts you can offer for a 12-month reserved commit vs a 6-month

Please attach any workload sizing template or quick TCO sketch. Procurement timeline: aim to finalize sizing by Nov 14 so we can move to contract review.

Emily
Emily Zhou
VP Product, HorizonBio
---
Attachment: horizonbio_workload_profile.xlsx (spreadsheet stub)

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T10:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Emily — thanks for the clear profile. High level answers up front, with a short sizing rationale below; attaching a lightweight workbook to show assumptions.

Short recommendation (initial commit):
- Baseline: 3 x NVIDIA H100-80GB (sustained pool) + 1 x H100 reserved for burst (hot spare) — effective pool = 4 but committed-to baseline = 3
- Expected sustained throughput: ~75–100 req/min at p95 < 1.2s for 8k contexts (depending on model variant). The burst spare covers short 6–12 minute peaks up to ~220 req/min when combined with autoscale and request queuing.
- If you need p95 < 0.7s for sustained traffic, move to 4x committed H100; that covers higher concurrency without relying on burst.

Sizing rationale / notes:
- Long context (8k) increases per-request compute and KV cache pressure; sequential attention scales roughly O(seq^2) for some kernels but our runtime uses chunked attention optimizations for long-context models — still, throughput drops ~35–50% vs 2k contexts.
- KV cache helps when many requests share recent prefixes; for HorizonBio workloads we saw limited cross-request prefix reuse, so we model only modest cache benefit (~10–15% effective throughput uplift).
- Batching: continuous batching with a 20–40ms aggregation window helps throughput without materially affecting p95 under moderate concurrency.
- Quantized model variants: recommend a 4-bit quantized medium-quality variant for non-clinical endpoints — ~25–35% throughput gain vs full-precision, with small quality delta. For clinical inference keep fp16 or bf16.

Commit / pricing guidance:
- 12-month reserved commit (3x H100 baseline): estimated list discount 15% — final depends on committed monthly hours / token floors.
- 6-month: ~10% discount.
- We can offer an 18% effective discount for 12-month if you include a minimum monthly token floor (we'll sketch numbers in the workbook).

Attachments: workload-sizing-summary.csv (assumptions + per-GPU throughput table).

Happy to walk through numbers—I can propose a formal sizing doc + SLA draft if this aligns.

—Alex
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, HorizonBio Procurement <procurement@horizonbio.com>
Date: 2025-11-05T08:50:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Alex — this is helpful. A few follow-ups: 
- Can you share a cost estimate (monthly $) for the 3x-committed + 1 burst spare config vs 4x-committed? Put baseline and peak columns.
- For the 18% discount path you mentioned, what is the required monthly token floor (rough estimate)?
- Are quantized variants something we can toggle per-route so we can keep clinical endpoints at fp16 but non-clinical at 4-bit?
- We will need SOC2 evidence and a short SLA for uptime and latency before procurement signs off.

Also looping Procurement — we'll target Nov 14 for sizing sign-off but we may push to Nov 21 if SOC2 review takes longer.

Thanks,
Emily
---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2025-11-06T09:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — committed vs burst pricing and SLA

Emily — Ben here (account lead). Appreciate the profile — quick notes from pricing/legal side: 
- I'll share an SLA draft and a pricing counteroffer for HorizonBio to review (attached). The counteroffer assumes a 12-month commit and a minimum monthly token floor of 120M tokens to qualify for the 18% discount. If you anticipate <120M tokens/mo we'll likely land between 12–15% depending on other terms.
- Burst credits: we can provide a burst pool (billing at a higher metered rate when used) or a hard burst GPU (reserved but billable pro-rata). The burst pool model is more flexible but metered; the hard burst GPU is simpler for budgeting.
- Legal will need SOC2 docs; Laura I’ve looped you for the standard evidence pack.

Attachments: SLA-draft-Redwood-v1.pdf, pricing-counteroffer.xlsx

Thanks — I can jump on a call tomorrow to go over numbers.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2025-11-07T14:20:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — committed vs burst pricing and SLA (detailed sizing)

Emily / Ben — attached is a short CSV + inline summary with per-GPU performance assumptions and two recommended packaging options. Summary (numbers are estimates based on current model selector and H100 perf profile):

Option A — Cost-conscious hybrid (recommended initial pilot)
- Commit: 3 x H100-80GB (baseline) + 1 x burst spare (pro-rata billed when used)
- Sustained capacity: ~95 req/min at p95 < 1.2s for 8k context model (fp16)
- Peak handling: burst spare + request queuing covers short 6–12min spikes up to ~210–230 req/min; expect p95 to rise to 1.6–2.0s during highest peaks
- Estimated cost (net of 15% list discount): $62,000/month (commit cost) + metered burst usage (~$3k–$7k/month typical depending on peak profile)

Option B — Performance-first (slightly higher commit)
- Commit: 4 x H100-80GB (no metered burst)
- Sustained capacity: ~150 req/min at p95 < 0.9s for 8k contexts (fp16)
- Peak handling: headroom for 220–250 req/min with p95 under 1.0s; more predictable latency SLOs
- Estimated cost (net of 15% list discount): $80,500/month

Discount/terms negotiation notes:
- If you commit to a 12-month term with a 120M token monthly floor, we can do 18% off list and include 1 month of free burst credits at signing (applied to the first invoice).
- A 6-month term we typically price at ~10% discount and cannot include the free burst credits.

Technical ops notes: 
- KV cache: for HorizonBio payloads we recommend enabling per-session KV caching with TTL=20m for multi-step interactions. That yields a modest throughput uplift but primarily reduces cost for workflows with repeated prefixes.
- Autoscaling: we recommend a conservative autoscaling policy for the first 4 weeks (scale step 0.5 GPU, cooldown 90s) to avoid rapid oscillation.
- Model mix: keep clinical endpoints on fp16/bf16; route non-clinical interactive UX to a quantized 4-bit model behind a separate route to save cost. This routing is per-endpoint and available in config.

Attachments: workload-sizing-summary.csv (per-GPU throughput table + cost calc). Happy to run a short simulation if you can drop hourly traffic for a couple of representative days.

—Alex
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Subject: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Alex,

Thanks for taking this. Quick background: HorizonBio is evaluating Redwood Dedicated for our clinical-genomics assistant. We expect long contexts (mostly 8k tokens), mixed generation (summaries + structured outputs), and a variable request pattern. Here's our rough profile: 
- Average requests: 60/min sustained
- Peak requests: 220/min for 6–12 minute windows (concurrent users ~40–60)
- Avg tokens per request (input+output): ~3k
- Latency target: p95 < 1.5s, p99 < 2.5s

We need guidance on:
1) Recommended GPU pool (type and #) for a reserved commit that meets the latency targets for sustained and peak traffic
2) How burst credits or a burst pool would work with a reserved commit (tradeoffs, costs)
3) How context length / KV cache affects sizing and whether caching reduces commit needs
4) What discounts you can offer for a 12-month reserved commit vs a 6-month

Please attach any workload sizing template or quick TCO sketch. Procurement timeline: aim to finalize sizing by Nov 14 so we can move to contract review.

Emily
Emily Zhou
VP Product, HorizonBio

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@horizonbio.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2025-11-04T10:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated sizing ask — context length, concurrency, guarantees

Hi Emily — thanks for the clear profile. High level answers up front, with a short sizing rationale below; attaching a lightweight workbook to show assumptions.

Short recommendation (initial commit):
- Baseline: 3 x NVIDIA H100-80GB (sustained pool) + 1 x H100 reserved for burst (hot spare) — effective pool = 4 but committed-to baseline = 3
- Expected sustained throughput: ~75–100 req/min at p95 < 1.2s for 8k contexts (depending on model variant). The burst spare covers short 6–12 minute peaks up to ~220 req/min when combined with autoscale and request queuing.
- If you need p95 < 0.7s for sustained traffic, move to 4x committed H100; that covers higher concurrency without relying on burst.

Sizing rationale / notes:
- Long context (8k) increases per-request compute and KV cache pressure; sequential attention scales roughly O(seq^2) for some kernels but our runtime uses chunked attention optimizations for long-context models — still, throughput drops ~35–50% vs 2k contexts.
- KV cache helps when many requests share recent prefixes; for HorizonBio workloads we saw limited cross-request prefix reuse, so we model only modest cache benefit (~10–15% effective throughput uplift).
- Batching: continuous batching with a 20–40ms aggregation window helps throughput without materially affecting p95 under moderate concurrency.
- Quantized model variants: recommend a 4-bit quantized medium-quality variant for non-clinical endpoints — ~25–35% throughput gain vs full-precision, with small quality delta. For clinical inference keep fp16 or bf16.

Commit / pricing guidance:
- 12-month reserved commit (3x H100 baseline): estimated list discount 15% — final depends on committed monthly hours / token floors.
- 6-month: ~10% discount.
- We can offer an 18% effective discount for 12-month if you include a minimum monthly token floor (we'll sketch numbers in the workbook).

Attachments: workload-sizing-summary.csv (assumptions + per-GPU throughput table).

Happy to walk through numbers—I can propose a formal sizing doc + SLA draft if this aligns.

—Alex
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Also looping Procurement — we'll target Nov 14 for sizing sign-off but we may push to Nov 21 if SOC2 review takes longer.

title: Annual retainer invoicing cadence & drawdown ledger — Starling Health
preview:
From: Marta Ruiz <accounts-payable@starlinghealth.com>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 1 Nov 2025 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Question on retainer/invoice cadence for 2026 retainer

Hi Vanessa,

Quick one from AP as we finalize Opex for next year — we received your November invoice and the SOW shows a single annual retainer that should be drawn down monthly from our prepay balance. We want to align the ledger on our side so we can automate the drawdowns. Two quick asks: 1) can you confirm the monthly drawdown amount you expect against the invoice (i.e., how you’ll amortize the retainer), and 2) confirm whether Redwood will issue monthly statements or a single statement with a monthly ledger breakdown?

Also, for VAT mapping: do you need our VAT registration number on the invoice or just the PO? Our tax team flagged that some EU cross-border rules might affect reporting if we treat this as a single prepay vs. monthly bill.

Thanks,
Marta
Accounts Payable
Starling Health

Attachment: PO_Starling_2025-11.pdf (pdf)


From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
To: Marta Ruiz <accounts-payable@starlinghealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Nov 2025 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Question on retainer/invoice cadence for 2026 retainer

Hi Marta — thanks for the note and the PO. Short answers first: we amortize the annual retainer evenly across the twelve months unless you request a custom ladder; we can provide a monthly ledger/statement that shows the starting prepay balance, each draw, and remaining balance. VAT: please include your VAT registration number on the PO and we will mirror it on the invoice.

I’ve attached the proposed schedule and an example month-by-month ledger so your automation rules can map fields directly. If you prefer a fixed monthly invoice (e.g., 12 x fixed amounts) rather than one annual invoice with ledger lines, we can do that as well — it just changes the invoice document set we issue.

Proposed monthly drawdown: $41,666.67 (annual retainer $500,000).

Next steps: 1) confirm preferred invoice document type (single annual + ledger vs. 12 monthly invoices), 2) confirm VAT number to include, and 3) if you want any custom GL/ledger fields added to the PDF.

Cheers,
Vanessa Ortiz
Enterprise Sales 
Redwood Inference
Tel: +1 415 555 0198

Attachments: Starling_Retainer_Schedule_2026.pdf (pdf), Redwood_Invoice_2025-11-01.pdf (pdf)

Ben

- Option A (single invoice + monthly ledger): issue annual invoice dated Nov 1 for $500,000; attach a CSV ledger with 12 lines (01-Nov-2025 start balance = 500,000; first draw 01-Dec-2025 = 41,666.67; etc.). We will include Starling’s VAT registration on the invoice and flag the ledger CSV with the PO number for automated ingestion.
- Option B (12 monthly invoices): generate 12 sequential monthly invoices (Dec 2025 — Nov 2026) each for $41,666.67. Each invoice will reference the annual PO and include VAT where applicable.

We recommend Option A for lower document churn and clearer single-source-of-truth, but we will accommodate Option B if their ERP requires discrete invoices. Attached is the example CSV export and a sample ledger PDF. We can also publish the CSV to an SFTP location upon request.

title: Marketplace list-price holdback & partner crediting for private offer
preview:
From: Jenna Park <jenna.park@acme-retail.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement <procurement@acme-retail.com>
Date: Mon, 22 Sep 2025 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Marketplace pricing: list-price holdback and partner crediting

Hi Aditya / Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the team was impressed with throughput and the cost controls. We are moving faster than expected on the procurement side and want to get a Private Offer ready for AWS and a parallel path for GCP. A couple of asks from procurement before we put the PO in: 

- Confirmation of Redwood's approach to list-price \"holdback\" when provisioning through AWS Marketplace (they want an explicit model showing the list price vs. net-back to Redwood and to partners).
- How channel partner crediting (reseller passback) will be represented on the AWS Private Offer and whether that differs for GCP Marketplace.
- Any marketplace fees or handling fees we should put in the PO so finance can do a quick model.

We have an indicative commit: 500k ARR across AWS + GCP over the first 12 months (split TBD). Procurement has attached a short workbook with expected invoice cadence and some questions (Acme_PO_Details.xlsx). Can you send a sketch of the proposed economics and an example of the private-offer fields we should expect in the AWS template?

Goal: get aligned this week and lock in a signature meeting next Wednesday.

Thanks,
Jenna Park
Director, Cloud Procurement
Acme Retail

Attachments: Acme_PO_Details.xlsx


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jenna Park <jenna.park@acme-retail.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 22 Sep 2025 13:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Marketplace pricing: list-price holdback and partner crediting

Hi Jenna — really appreciate the fast follow.

High-level answers up front so procurement can model quickly:
- We publish a list price in the marketplace listing. For private offers, the PO will reference a list price but the net-back to Redwood is typically the list price minus marketplace fees and any partner/reseller passback.
- Typical split we see on deals this size is marketplace fee 6–9% (variable by cloud) + partner passback 8–15% depending on the partner tier and negotiated commission. The remaining net is Redwood revenue.
- For GCP the marketplace fee mechanics differ (usually an invoice credit model) — we'll attach an AWS private-offer template and a sample net-back worksheet.

I've attached a quick estimate and a sketch of a channel-split we think will be acceptable to Acme based on the commitment (redwood_po_pricing_v1.pdf). Proposed working model (for illustration only):
- List price: $1,000/unit (example)
- AWS marketplace fee: 7%
- Partner passback: 12%
- Redwood net-back: 81% (post-fee/passback)

If that looks directionally right, we can convert to a per-SKU model and generate an AWS Private Offer draft (we will populate the AWS template fields that procurement needs). Can we hop on a 30-min sync tomorrow morning to get the commit split and partner identity?

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0172

Attachments: redwood_po_pricing_v1.pdf


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 23 Sep 2025 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Marketplace pricing: list-price holdback and partner crediting

Forwarding Jenna's PO workbook — procurement wants explicit line items for marketplace holdback and partner credits.

Kimberly — quick thoughts from rev ops: we should avoid presenting the partner passback as a line-item that reduces the list price on the marketplace entry itself. That creates visibility and could trigger unexpected tax or resale implications on Acme's side. Instead:
- Use list price as the canonical marketplace SKU price.
- Represent partner passback as a contractual rebate (settled monthly/quarterly) or as a pass-through line on the private-offer terms that is visible to Acme and AWS but not as a permanent list-price reduction.

Also: watch for tax treatment on passbacks — if the partner is receiving a passback directly from Redwood it may change invoicing. If the partner is paid by Acme (e.g., reseller rebate) the PO mechanics differ.

Ben
VP, Marketplace Sales | Redwood Inference

--- Forwarded message from Jenna Park <jenna.park@acme-retail.com> ---
(Attached: Acme_PO_Details.xlsx)


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 23 Sep 2025 10:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Marketplace pricing: list-price holdback and partner crediting

Ben — agreed on the rebate vs list-price visibility approach. A couple of operational notes for Aditya / Jenna: 

- AWS Private Offer fields: we'll include an \"Invoice Adjustment\" field in our internal PO mapping and then a separate contractual appendix that documents the partner passback percentage and payment cadence. That lets Acme see the net economics in one place while keeping marketplace listing simple.
- Recommended starting point for channel splits (given the $500k blended commit): marketplace fee 7% (assume AWS), partner passback 10% (proposal), Redwood net-back 83%. If Acme insists on a partner-visible line we can map it to a periodic rebate.
- For GCP, recommend we model fees as credits because GCP doesn't flow marketplace fees the same way; treat it as an account-level credit on the invoice.

I'll attach the AWS Private Offer template and a drive reference showing how we map line-items for finance.

Kimberly Park
Director, Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com

Attachments: AWS_Private_Offer_Template.docx
Drive: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1a2b3cAcmePORef


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jenna Park <jenna.park@acme-retail.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, procurement <procurement@acme-retail.com>, AWS Marketplace <aws-marketplace@amazon.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Sep 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Marketplace pricing: list-price holdback & partner crediting for private offer

Hi Jenna — thanks for sending the workbook. Per our internal ops review (Ben / Kimberly looped in), here's a proposed, actionable structure you can give to finance and AWS: 

1) List price: Redwood will publish the SKU at the list price referenced in the PO (we used the example in our sketch).
2) Marketplace fees: estimate at 7% for AWS; GCP modeled as invoice credit (we'll provide an example).
3) Partner passback: proposed 10% of list price, paid as a quarterly rebate from Redwood to the named partner. We will not reduce the marketplace list price to reflect the partner passback; instead, we will document the passback in the private-offer appendix and in the contract so Acme's finance team sees the net economics.
4) Tax/Invoice handling: if Acme wants partner paid directly by Redwood, that will require Redwood to issue a separate rebate invoice and could change VAT/sales tax treatment. If Acme prefers partner paid by Acme via a separate line in their internal procurement flows, that's fine and simpler for tax treatment.

Attached: redwood_po_pricing_v1.pdf (illustrative net-back table) and AWS_Private_Offer_Template.docx (fields we will fill when we create the private offer).

Next steps:
- Can we lock a 30-min call today (3pm PT) or tomorrow morning to confirm the partner identity and exact commit split between AWS and GCP?
- If procurement wants to proceed to signature, we'll draft the Private Offer and a short appendix describing the passback mechanics and tax options.

Looking forward —
Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

- Any marketplace fees or handling fees we should put in the PO so finance can do a quick model.

Acme Retail

Subject: Re: Marketplace pricing: list-price holdback and partner crediting

Hi Jenna — really appreciate the fast follow.

High-level answers up front so procurement can model quickly:
- We publish a list price in the marketplace listing. For private offers, the PO will reference a list price but the net-back to Redwood is typically the list price minus marketplace fees and any partner/reseller passback.
- Typical split we see on deals this size is marketplace fee 6–9% (variable by cloud) + partner passback 8–15% depending on the partner tier and negotiated commission. The remaining net is Redwood revenue.
- For GCP the marketplace fee mechanics differ (usually an invoice credit model) — we'll attach an AWS private-offer template and a sample net-back worksheet.

I've attached a quick estimate and a sketch of a channel-split we think will be acceptable to Acme based on the commitment (redwood_po_pricing_v1.pdf). Proposed working model (for illustration only):
- List price: $1,000/unit (example)
- AWS marketplace fee: 7%
- Partner passback: 12%
- Redwood net-back: 81% (post-fee/passback)

Subject: Fwd: Marketplace pricing: list-price holdback and partner crediting

Forwarding Jenna's PO workbook — procurement wants explicit line items for marketplace holdback and partner credits.

Kimberly — quick thoughts from rev ops: we should avoid presenting the partner passback as a line-item that reduces the list price on the marketplace entry itself. That creates visibility and could trigger unexpected tax or resale implications on Acme's side. Instead:
- Use list price as the canonical marketplace SKU price.

title: Clarifying data residency & deletion runbook for non-training assurance
preview:
From: Samir Chaudhry <samir@finhealth.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: FinHealth Security <security@finhealth.com>, procurement@finhealth.com
Date: Wed, 10 Sep 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Quick question on ‘no training’ claim + backups/replicas

Alex —

Thanks for the demo yesterday. Our security team needs a short write-up confirming two things before we proceed: 1) that Redwood does not use our tenant data to train any public or internal foundation models; and 2) exactly how long debug snapshots, backups, and cross-region replicas are retained after a tenant requests deletion.

Specifically: if FinHealth decides to stop using the service and requests full deletion, what happens to:
- KV caches/prefix caches used for inference performance;
- ephemeral debug snapshots captured for incident triage;
- backups and cross-region replicas (are they tombstoned immediately, or retained for a window?), and
- any telemetry that might be aggregated for platform improvement.

We need something concise we can attach to the procurement packet (1 page preferred).

Thanks,
Samir
Samir Chaudhry
Head of Security, FinHealth


From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
To: Samir Chaudhry <samir@finhealth.com>
Cc: FinHealth Security <security@finhealth.com>, Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Sep 2025 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick question on ‘no training’ claim + backups/replicas

Samir —

Great questions. Short answer: we do not use customer tenant content to train our public foundation models. For the retention bullets you listed I want to loop in Mina (privacy/ops) to give exact windows and the deletion runbook steps.

I'll follow up with a one-page attestation plus a short runbook snippet that you can include in procurement. Expect a doc and a summary in the next 48 hours.

Best,
Alex Martinez
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>
To: Samir Chaudhry <samir@finhealth.com>, Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Sep 2025 11:23:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Quick question on ‘no training’ claim + backups/replicas

Hi Samir —

Thanks for the clear list. I outlined the operational behavior below; I'll attach the shorter attestation you requested and a matrix that maps each artifact to retention/erase behavior. If you want an even shorter single-sentence attestation for procurement, we can provide that as well.

Key points (operational detail):
- Training usage: Redwood does not feed tenant content into any general-purpose training pipeline for public or internal foundation models. We may use non-identifying, aggregated telemetry (e.g., request volume, error rates, latency histograms) to improve platform infrastructure; customers can opt out of telemetry aggregation per contract.

- KV cache / prefix cache: ephemeral caches are in-memory within the serving cluster and are invalidated on delete requests. For safety we mark the tenant data as deleted immediately and invalidate keys; any in-flight cache entries are evicted within the next maintenance window (normally < 24 hours).

- Debug snapshots / incident triage artifacts: we create snapshots only when debugging a specific issue. Those snapshots are tagged, access-controlled, and have a 30-day retention by default. On a tenant deletion request we run the delete-runbook: snapshots are moved to a tombstone state immediately and scheduled for irreversible purge after a 30-day retention window unless an active legal hold is raised. We can shorten the snapshot purging window contractually to 0 days for regulated customers (enterprise add-on).

- Backups & cross-region replicas: backups are retained for disaster recovery for a rolling 30-day period by default. Cross-region replicas are asynchronous and are considered part of backups for deletion purposes: when deletion is requested we trigger tombstone markers across all replica regions and schedule a purge job. Purge completes in a rolling process; typical completion is 30–45 days depending on dataset size and snapshot backlog. We provide attestations when purge completes.

- Telemetry and aggregated metrics: platform telemetry used for improving performance is generally aggregated and non-identifying. Customers who require zero telemetry retention can opt out; this is included as a line item in our Dedicated/Private contracts.

Actionable items we can provide you now: a one-page non-training attestation, the data retention matrix (attached), and a short delete-runbook summary (attached). If procurement needs the snapshots purge shortened we can propose add-on language for the SOW.

Regards,
Mina Haddad
Head of Privacy & Product Security
Redwood Inference


From: Markus Klein <markus@redwood.com>
To: Samir Chaudhry <samir@finhealth.com>, Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>, Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: FinHealth Security <security@finhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Sep 2025 08:49:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Quick question on ‘no training’ claim + backups/replicas

Samir / team —

Two legal clarifications to help procurement: 1) we can commit contractually that \"Redwood will not use Customer Content to train foundation models,\" and 2) retention windows (snapshots/backups) are operationally described as Mina noted. If FinHealth needs expedited purge (e.g., reduction from 30 days to 0 days), we require the SOW addendum and acknowledgement of increased operational risk for debugging/recovery.

I'll drop suggested redline language for the Master Services Agreement and a short addendum for the retention carveout into the HubSpot record. Alex — can you confirm the procurement contact so we route the DocuSign correctly?

—Markus
Markus Klein
VP Legal, Redwood Inference

From: Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>
To: Samir Chaudhry <samir@finhealth.com>, Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Sep 2025 11:23:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Quick question on ‘no training’ claim + backups/replicas

Hi Samir —

Thanks for the clear list. I outlined the operational behavior below; I'll attach the shorter attestation you requested and a matrix that maps each artifact to retention/erase behavior. If you want an even shorter single-sentence attestation for procurement, we can provide that as well.

Key points (operational detail):
- Training usage: Redwood does not feed tenant content into any general-purpose training pipeline for public or internal foundation models. We may use non-identifying, aggregated telemetry (e.g., request volume, error rates, latency histograms) to improve platform infrastructure; customers can opt out of telemetry aggregation per contract.

- KV cache / prefix cache: ephemeral caches are in-memory within the serving cluster and are invalidated on delete requests. For safety we mark the tenant data as deleted immediately and invalidate keys; any in-flight cache entries are evicted within the next maintenance window (normally < 24 hours).

- Debug snapshots / incident triage artifacts: we create snapshots only when debugging a specific issue. Those snapshots are tagged, access-controlled, and have a 30-day retention by default. On a tenant deletion request we run the delete-runbook: snapshots are moved to a tombstone state immediately and scheduled for irreversible purge after a 30-day retention window unless an active legal hold is raised. We can shorten the snapshot purging window contractually to 0 days for regulated customers (enterprise add-on).

- Backups & cross-region replicas: backups are retained for disaster recovery for a rolling 30-day period by default. Cross-region replicas are asynchronous and are considered part of backups for deletion purposes: when deletion is requested we trigger tombstone markers across all replica regions and schedule a purge job. Purge completes in a rolling process; typical completion is 30–45 days depending on dataset size and snapshot backlog. We provide attestations when purge completes.

- Telemetry and aggregated metrics: platform telemetry used for improving performance is generally aggregated and non-identifying. Customers who require zero telemetry retention can opt out; this is included as a line item in our Dedicated/Private contracts.

Actionable items we can provide you now: a one-page non-training attestation, the data retention matrix (attached), and a short delete-runbook summary (attached). If procurement needs the snapshots purge shortened we can propose add-on language for the SOW.

Regards,
Mina Haddad
Head of Privacy & Product Security
Redwood Inference
